Executes a given bash command in a persistent shell session with optional timeout, ensuring proper handling and security measures.

Be aware: OS: linux, Shell: bash

All commands run in the current working directory by default. Use the `workdir` parameter if you need to run a command in a different directory. AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>` patterns - use `workdir` instead.

Use `/tmp/opencode` for temporary work outside the workspace. This directory has already been created, already exists, and is pre-approved for external directory access.

IMPORTANT: This tool is for terminal operations like git, npm, docker, etc. DO NOT use it for file operations (reading, writing, editing, searching, finding files) - use the specialized tools for this instead.

Before executing the command, please follow these steps:

1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory

2. Command Execution:
   - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes (e.g., rm "path with spaces/file.txt")
   - Examples of proper quoting:
     - mkdir "/Users/name/My Documents" (correct)
     - mkdir /Users/name/My Documents (incorrect - will fail)
     - python "/path/with spaces/script.py" (correct)
     - python /path/with spaces/script.py (incorrect - will fail)
   - After ensuring proper quoting, execute the command.
   - Capture the output of the command.

Usage notes:
  - The command argument is required.
  - You can specify an optional timeout in milliseconds. If not specified, commands will time out after 120000ms (2 minutes).
  - It is very helpful if you write a clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words.
  - If the output exceeds 2000 lines or 51200 bytes, it will be truncated and the full output will be written to a file. You can use Read with offset/limit to read specific sections or Grep to search the full content. Do NOT use `head`, `tail`, or other truncation commands to limit output; the full output will already be captured to a file for more precise searching.

  - Avoid using Bash with the `find`, `grep`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `sed`, `awk`, or `echo` commands, unless explicitly instructed or when these commands are truly necessary for the task. Instead, always prefer using the dedicated tools for these commands:
    - File search: Use Glob (NOT find or ls)
    - Content search: Use Grep (NOT grep or rg)
    - Read files: Use Read (NOT cat/head/tail)
    - Edit files: Use Edit (NOT sed/awk)
    - Write files: Use Write (NOT echo >/cat <<EOF)
    - Communication: Output text directly (NOT echo/printf)
  - When issuing multiple commands:
    - If the commands are independent and can run in parallel, make multiple Bash tool calls in a single message. For example, if you need to run "git status" and "git diff", send a single message with two Bash tool calls in parallel.
    - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together (e.g., `git add . && git commit -m "message" && git push`). For instance, if one operation must complete before another starts (like mkdir before cp, Write before Bash for git operations, or git add before git commit), run these operations sequentially instead.
    - Use ';' only when you need to run commands sequentially but don't care if earlier commands fail
    - DO NOT use newlines to separate commands (newlines are ok in quoted strings)
  - AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>`. Use the `workdir` parameter to change directories instead.
    <good-example>
    Use workdir="/foo/bar" with command: pytest tests
    </good-example>
    <bad-example>
    cd /foo/bar && pytest tests

# Committing changes with git

Only create commits when requested by the user. If unclear, ask first. When the user asks you to create a new git commit, follow these steps carefully:

Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- Avoid git commit --amend. ONLY use --amend when ALL conditions are met:
  (1) User explicitly requested amend, OR the commit succeeded and pre-commit hooks auto-modified files that need including — verify by checking `git log` that HEAD is the new commit before amending
  (2) HEAD commit was created by you in this conversation (verify: git log -1 --format='%an %ae')
  (3) Commit has NOT been pushed to remote (verify: git status shows "Your branch is ahead")
- CRITICAL: If commit FAILED or was REJECTED by hook, NEVER amend - fix the issue and create a NEW commit
- CRITICAL: If you already pushed to remote, NEVER amend unless user explicitly requests it (requires force push)
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.

1. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following bash commands in parallel, each using the Bash tool:
  - Run a git status command to see all untracked files.
  - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed.
  - Run a git log command to see recent commit messages, so that you can follow this repository's commit message style.
2. Analyze all staged changes (both previously staged and newly added) and draft a commit message:
  - Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
  - Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc.). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
  - Draft a concise (1-2 sentences) commit message that focuses on the "why" rather than the "what"
  - Ensure it accurately reflects the changes and their purpose
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands:
   - Add relevant untracked files to the staging area.
   - Create the commit with a message
   - Run git status after the commit completes to verify success.
   Note: git status depends on the commit completing, so run it sequentially after the commit.
4. If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (see amend rules above)

Important notes:
- NEVER run additional commands to read or explore code, besides git bash commands
- NEVER use the TodoWrite or Task tools
- DO NOT push to the remote repository unless the user explicitly asks you to do so
- IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
- If there are no changes to commit (i.e., no untracked files and no modifications), do not create an empty commit

2. Analyze all changes that will be included in the pull request, making sure to look at all relevant commits (NOT just the latest commit, but ALL commits that will be included in the pull request!!!), and draft a pull request summary
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands in parallel:

Use this tool to create and manage a structured task list for your current coding session. This helps you track progress, organize complex tasks, and demonstrate thoroughness to the user.
It also helps the user understand the progress of the task and overall progress of their requests.

## When to Use This Tool
Use this tool proactively in these scenarios:

1. Complex multistep tasks - When a task requires 3 or more distinct steps or actions
2. Non-trivial and complex tasks - Tasks that require careful planning or multiple operations
3. User explicitly requests todo list - When the user directly asks you to use the todo list
4. User provides multiple tasks - When users provide a list of things to be done (numbered or comma-separated)
5. After receiving new instructions - Immediately capture user requirements as todos. Feel free to edit the todo list based on new information.
6. After completing a task - Mark it complete and add any new follow-up tasks
7. When you start working on a new task, mark the todo as in_progress. Ideally you should only have one todo as in_progress at a time. Complete existing tasks before starting new ones.

## When NOT to Use This Tool

Skip using this tool when:
1. There is only a single, straightforward task
2. The task is trivial and tracking it provides no organizational benefit
3. The task can be completed in less than 3 trivial steps
4. The task is purely conversational or informational

NOTE that you should not use this tool if there is only one trivial task to do. In this case you are better off just doing the task directly.

## Examples of When to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: I want to add a dark mode toggle to the application settings. Make sure you run the tests and build when you're done!
Assistant: I'll help add a dark mode toggle to your application settings. Let me create a todo list to track this implementation.
*Creates todo list with the following items:*
1. Create dark mode toggle component in Settings page
2. Add dark mode state management (context/store)
3. Implement CSS-in-JS styles for dark theme
4. Update existing components to support theme switching
5. Run tests and build process, addressing any failures or errors that occur
*Begins working on the first task*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. Adding dark mode is a multi-step feature requiring UI, state management, and styling changes
2. The user explicitly requested tests and build be run afterward
3. The assistant inferred that tests and build need to pass by adding "Ensure tests and build succeed" as the final task
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Help me rename the function getCwd to getCurrentWorkingDirectory across my project
Assistant: Let me first search through your codebase to find all occurrences of 'getCwd'.
*Uses grep or search tools to locate all instances of getCwd in the codebase*
Assistant: I've found 15 instances of 'getCwd' across 8 different files. Let me create a todo list to track these changes.
*Creates todo list with specific items for each file that needs updating*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant searched to understand the scope of the task
2. Upon finding multiple occurrences across different files, it determined this was a complex task with multiple steps
3. The todo list helps ensure every instance is tracked and updated systematically
4. This approach prevents missing any occurrences and maintains code consistency
</reasoning>
</example>


<example>
User: I need to implement these features for my e-commerce site: user registration, product catalog, shopping cart, and checkout flow.
Assistant: I'll help implement these features. First, let's add all the features to the todo list.
*Creates a todo list breaking down each feature into specific tasks based on the project architecture*
Assistant: Let's start with user registration. This will involve creating database models, API endpoints, and frontend forms that integrate with your existing authentication system.

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. The user provided multiple complex features to implement in a comma separated list
2. The todo list helps organize these large features into manageable tasks
3. This approach allows for tracking progress across the entire implementation
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you help optimize my React application? It's rendering slowly and has performance issues.</user>
Assistant: I'll help optimize your React application. First, let me examine your codebase to identify potential performance bottlenecks.
*Reviews component structure, render patterns, state management, and data fetching*
Assistant: After analyzing your codebase, I've identified several performance issues. Let me create a todo list to track our optimization efforts.
*Creates todo list with items like: 1) Implement memoization for expensive calculations in ProductList, 2) Add virtualization for long lists in Dashboard, 3) Optimize image loading in Gallery component, 4) Fix state update loops in ShoppingCart, 5) Review bundle size and implement code splitting*
Let's start by implementing memoization for the expensive calculations in your ProductList component.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant examined the codebase to identify specific performance issues
2. Based on this analysis, it identified multiple optimization opportunities
3. Performance optimization is a non-trivial task requiring multiple steps
4. The todo list helps methodically track improvements across different components
5. This systematic approach ensures all performance bottlenecks are addressed
</reasoning>
</example>

## Examples of When NOT to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: How do I print 'Hello World' in Python?
Assistant: In Python, you can print "Hello World" with this simple code:

python
print("Hello World")

This will output the text "Hello World" to the console when executed.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, trivial task that can be completed in one step. There's no need to track multiple tasks or steps for such a straightforward request.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: What does the git status command do?
Assistant: The git status command shows the current state of your working directory and staging area. It displays which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked by Git. This is a fundamental command for understanding the current state of your repository before making commits.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is an informational request with no actual coding task to complete. The user is simply asking for an explanation, not for the assistant to perform multiple steps or tasks.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does?
Assistant: Sure, let me add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does.
* Uses the Edit tool to add a comment to the calculateTotal function *

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, straightforward task confined to one location in the code. Adding a comment doesn't require tracking multiple steps or systematic organization.

Assistant: I'll run the npm install command for you.

The command completed successfully. Here's the output:

All dependencies have been installed according to your package.json file.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single command execution with immediate results. There are no multiple steps to track or organize, making the todo list unnecessary for this straightforward task.
</reasoning>
</example>

## Task States and Management

1. **Task States**: Use these states to track progress:
   - pending: Task not yet started
   - in_progress: Currently working on (limit to ONE task at a time)
   - completed: Task finished successfully
   - cancelled: Task no longer needed

2. **Task Management**:
   - Update task status in real-time as you work
   - Mark tasks complete IMMEDIATELY after finishing (don't batch completions)
   - Only have ONE task in_progress at any time
   - Complete current tasks before starting new ones
   - Cancel tasks that become irrelevant

3. **Task Breakdown**:
   - Create specific, actionable items
   - Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
   - Use clear, descriptive task names

When in doubt, use this tool. Being proactive with task management demonstrates attentiveness and ensures you complete all requirements successfully.

data.content:
# Write Cypher

## Overview

Use this skill for VAST DB Cypher query patterns. Prefer reusable templates, and construct new queries when no template matches the analysis need.

## Workflow

1. Read `references/query-templates.md`. If a template matches the task, reuse it as-is.
2. If no template matches, construct a new Cypher query using the VAST DB schema and the closest related template as guidance.

## Query Strategy

- Load `understand-mlir-schema` when query construction or result interpretation depends on schema details.
- Use small exploratory queries with `LIMIT` when schema details such as operation names, properties, node and relationship labels are uncertain.
- When starting from a source location, first map it to operation `uid`s. Use those `uid`s as stable handles for follow-up queries such as enclosing functions, call relationships, and def-use data flow.
- Return only properties needed for the analysis. Common fields are node `uid`, relationship `idx`, operation `name`, `location`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `className`, type `type`, and attribute `value`.
- Order indexed relationships by `idx`.

## Query Rules

- Generate read-only queries by default. Do not use `CREATE`, `MERGE`, `SET`, `DELETE`, or `REMOVE`.
- Use parameter placeholders such as `$func_name`, `$op_uid`, `$file_name`, `$line`, and `$callee`.
- Do not invent node and relationship labels, properties, or operation names. Verify unfamiliar schema details with `understand-mlir-schema` or exploratory queries.

## Common Patterns

Read `references/query-templates.md` for reusable Cypher templates.

Use this before writing Cypher queries against VAST DB. For query patterns, load write-cypher.

# Understand MLIR Schema

## Overview

This skill describes the VAST DB schema. Use it to check node labels, relationship types, and properties before writing or interpreting Cypher queries. Do not guess labels or relationships; look them up here.

## Core Schema

### Node Labels

- `OPERATIONS` — MLIR operations. Properties include `uid`, `name`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `mod_name`, and `location`. The `location` field has the form `['F', file, line, column]`.
- `VALUES` — SSA values. Only property is `uid`.
- `INTATTRS` — Integer attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (int), `attr_type` (int).
- `FLOATATTRS` — Float attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (float), `attr_type` (int).
- `STRINGATTRS` — String attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (string), `attr_type` (int).
- `TYPES` — MLIR types. Properties: `uid`, `type` (type mnemonic), `has_dl` (bool), `bw` (int bitwidth), `align` (int), `record_name` (string, for hl.record only).
- `REGIONS` — MLIR regions. Properties: `uid`, `regType` (bool: true for GraphRegion, false for SSACFG).
- `BLOCKS` — MLIR blocks. Only property is `uid`.
- `VTABLES` — Virtual tables. Properties: `uid`, `vtable_name`, `components_flat`, `components_sizes`.
- `VTTS` — VTT (virtual table table). Properties: `uid`, `vtt_name`, `components_flat`.

### Key Relationships

Operation operands and results:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation consumes a value as operand
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:RESVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation produces a value as result
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGATTR {idx}]->(:INTATTRS|FLOATATTRS|STRINGATTRS)` — operation has an attribute
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — operation references a block (e.g., branch targets)
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGTYPE {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — operation has a type operand

Containment (operation bodies):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:CHILDREGION {idx}]->(:REGIONS)` — operation contains a region
- `(:REGIONS)-[:CHILDBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — region contains a block
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:CHILDINST {idx}]->(:OPERATIONS)` — block contains an instruction (operation)
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:BLOCKARG {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — block has a block argument

Type system:
- `(:VALUES)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — value has a type
- `(:TYPES)-[:ARG {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — composite type has component types
- `(:INTATTRS|:FLOATATTRS)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — non-string attribute has a type

Function and variable types:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:FUNCTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global function's type signature
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:VARTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global variable's type

Virtual tables (only from `builtin.module` operations):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTABLE]->(:VTABLES)` — module has a vtable
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTT]->(:VTTS)` — module has a VTT

## References

- `references/schema-mlir-graph.md` — canonical node labels, relationships, properties, constraints, and example queries.
- `references/operations-catalog.md` — every operation name (`hl.func`, `hl.call`, `ll.load`, etc.) with its specific properties and relationships.
- `references/types-catalog.md` — every type mnemonic (`hl.int`, `i32`, `core.fn`, etc.) with its properties and subtype structure.

I see, I don't have a bash tool. Let me just use what I can read. Let me compare the files I already read.

The only difference between bad.c and good.c is at line 672:
- bad.c line 672: `if (dynrelcount < 0)`
- good.c line 672: `if (dynrelcount <= 0)`

This is the fix. The vulnerability is that when `dynrelcount == 0`, `_bfd_elf_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc` still sets `*storage = NULL` at line 622 (as a sentinel), so `dynrelbuf[0]` is NULL. Then at line 849, `p = dynrelbuf[0]` sets p to NULL. The binary search condition `(min+1) < max` with min=0, max=0 is false, so the loop body doesn't execute. At line 868, `p->address` dereferences NULL.

Both agents correctly identify the NULL pointer dereference from the sentinel through the binary search path, but neither mentions the actual fix change at line 672. Both have minor imprecision about "no match" scenario.

Let me provide my judgement.

## Root Cause

In `_bfd_elf_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc` (line 622), `*storage = NULL` sets a NULL sentinel to terminate the relocation array. Later, in `_bfd_elf_get_synthetic_symtab`, this array is used for binary search. At line 849, `p` is initialized to `dynrelbuf[0]`, which will be NULL if there are no relocations (the array only contains the sentinel). When the binary search doesn't find a matching relocation (the `while` loop at lines 852-865 never sets `p` to a matching element), `p` remains pointing to the NULL sentinel. The condition at line 868 checks `got_vma == p->address` first, which dereferences `p` when it points to NULL, causing the null pointer dereference.

   - Calls `bfd_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc` which invokes `_bfd_elf_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc`, populating `dynrelbuf` with relocations followed by a NULL sentinel

   - Performs binary search through `dynrelbuf` to find matching relocation by address. At line 849, `p = dynrelbuf[0]` initializes p to first element, which is NULL when `dynrelcount` is 0.    - The condition `got_vma == p->address` dereferences `p` when it points to NULL, triggering the null pointer dereference.

metadata.preview:
Now I have enough information from both code inspection and VAST DB to construct the analysis. Let me provide the final answer.

## Root Cause

The vulnerability is a **CWE-476 Null Pointer Dereference** in the BFD library's ELF dynamic relocation handling. The root cause is that `_bfd_elf_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc` (line 622) sets `*storage = NULL` as a sentinel value to terminate the relocation array. However, `_bfd_elf_get_synthetic_symtab` passes `dynrelbuf` to `bfd_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc` and later performs a binary search on this buffer. When the binary search does not find a matching relocation entry (e.g., due to zero relocations or no match), it exits the loop with `p` potentially pointing to an invalid or NULL element. The sink check at line 868 then accesses `p->address` and `p->howto` without properly validating that `p` points to a valid entry, triggering the null pointer dereference.

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:592` `_bfd_elf_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc`
   - Called by `bfd_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc` macro (line 452-453) which is invoked from `_bfd_elf_get_synthetic_symtab` at line 671.
   - Populates the `storage` array with relocation entries and sets `*storage = NULL` at line 622 to terminate the array.

2. `bad.c:671` `_bfd_elf_get_synthetic_symtab`
   - Calls `bfd_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc(abfd, dynrelbuf, dynsyms)` which fills `dynrelbuf` with relocation entries followed by a NULL sentinel.
   - The returned `dynrelcount` indicates the number of valid entries, but the NULL sentinel exists at `dynrelbuf[dynrelcount]`.

3. `bad.c:849-865` `_bfd_elf_get_synthetic_symtab` (binary search loop)
   - Initializes `p = dynrelbuf[0]` at line 849 and performs binary search.
   - If `dynrelcount` is 0 or no match is found, the loop may exit without properly validating `p`.

591: /* Original code from bad.c */
592: long _bfd_elf_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc(bfd *abfd, arelent **storage,
593:                                          asymbol **syms) {
594:   bfd_boolean (*slurp_relocs)(bfd *, asection *, asymbol **, bfd_boolean);
595:   asection *s;
596:   long ret;
597: 
598:   if (elf_dynsymtab(abfd) == 0) {
599:     bfd_set_error(bfd_error_invalid_operation);
600:     return -1;
601:   }
602: 
603:   slurp_relocs = get_elf_backend_data(abfd)->s->slurp_reloc_table;
604:   ret = 0;
605:   for (s = abfd->sections; s != NULL; s = s->next) {
606:     if (elf_section_data(s)->this_hdr.sh_link == elf_dynsymtab(abfd) &&
607:         (elf_section_data(s)->this_hdr.sh_type == SHT_REL ||
608:          elf_section_data(s)->this_hdr.sh_type == SHT_RELA)) {
609:       arelent *p;
610:       long count, i;
611: 
612:       if (!(*slurp_relocs)(abfd, s, syms, TRUE))
613:         return -1;
614:       count = s->size / elf_section_data(s)->this_hdr.sh_entsize;
615:       p = s->relocation;
616:       for (i = 0; i < count; i++)
617:         *storage++ = p++;
618:       ret += count;
619:     }
620:   }
621: 
622:   *storage = NULL;
623: 
624:   return ret;
625: }
626: 
627: static long _bfd_elf_get_synthetic_symtab(bfd *abfd,
628:                                           long symcount ATTRIBUTE_UNUSED,
629:                                           asymbol **syms ATTRIBUTE_UNUSED,
630:                                           long dynsymcount, asymbol **dynsyms,
631:                                           asymbol **ret) {
632:   long size, count, i, n;
633:   int j;
634:   unsigned int plt_got_offset, plt_entry_size, plt_got_insn_size;
635:   asymbol *s;
636:   bfd_byte *plt_contents;
637:   long dynrelcount, relsize;
638:   arelent **dynrelbuf;
639:   const struct elf_x86_64_lazy_plt_layout *lazy_plt;
640:   const struct elf_x86_64_non_lazy_plt_layout *non_lazy_plt;
641:   const struct elf_x86_64_lazy_plt_layout *lazy_bnd_plt;
642:   const struct elf_x86_64_non_lazy_plt_layout *non_lazy_bnd_plt;
643:   const struct elf_x86_64_lazy_plt_layout *lazy_ibt_plt;
644:   const struct elf_x86_64_non_lazy_plt_layout *non_lazy_ibt_plt;
645:   asection *plt;
646:   char *names;
647:   enum elf_x86_64_plt_type plt_type;
648:   struct elf_x86_64_plt plts[] = {
649:       {".plt", NULL, NULL, plt_unknown, 0, 0, 0, 0},
650:       {".plt.got", NULL, NULL, plt_non_lazy, 0, 0, 0, 0},
651:       {".plt.sec", NULL, NULL, plt_second, 0, 0, 0, 0},
652:       {".plt.bnd", NULL, NULL, plt_second, 0, 0, 0, 0},
653:       {NULL, NULL, NULL, plt_non_lazy, 0, 0, 0, 0}};
654: 
655:   *ret = NULL;
656: 
657:   if ((abfd->flags & (DYNAMIC | EXEC_P)) == 0)
658:     return 0;
659: 
660:   if (dynsymcount <= 0)
661:     return 0;
662: 
663:   relsize = bfd_get_dynamic_reloc_upper_bound(abfd);
664:   if (relsize <= 0)
665:     return -1;
666: 
667:   dynrelbuf = (arelent **)bfd_malloc(relsize);
668:   if (dynrelbuf == NULL)
669:     return -1;
670: 
671:   dynrelcount = bfd_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc(abfd, dynrelbuf, dynsyms);
672:   if (dynrelcount < 0)
673:     return -1;
674: 
675:   /* Sort the relocs by address.  */
676:   qsort(dynrelbuf, dynrelcount, sizeof(arelent *), compare_relocs);
677: 
678:   if (get_elf_x86_64_backend_data(abfd)->os == is_normal) {
679:     lazy_plt = &elf_x86_64_lazy_plt;
680:     non_lazy_plt = &elf_x86_64_non_lazy_plt;
681:     lazy_bnd_plt = &elf_x86_64_lazy_bnd_plt;
682:     non_lazy_bnd_plt = &elf_x86_64_non_lazy_bnd_plt;
683:     if (ABI_64_P(abfd)) {
684:       lazy_ibt_plt = &elf_x86_64_lazy_ibt_plt;
685:       non_lazy_ibt_plt = &elf_x86_64_non_lazy_ibt_plt;
686:     } else {
687:       lazy_ibt_plt = &elf_x32_lazy_ibt_plt;
688:       non_lazy_ibt_plt = &elf_x32_non_lazy_ibt_plt;
689:     }
690:   } else {
691:     lazy_plt = &elf_x86_64_nacl_plt;
692:     non_lazy_plt = NULL;
693:     lazy_bnd_plt = NULL;
694:     non_lazy_bnd_plt = NULL;
695:     lazy_ibt_plt = NULL;
696:     non_lazy_ibt_plt = NULL;
697:   }
698: 
699:   count = 0;
700:   for (j = 0; plts[j].name != NULL; j++) {
701:     plt = bfd_get_section_by_name(abfd, plts[j].name);
702:     if (plt == NULL || plt->size == 0)
703:       continue;
704: 
705:     /* Get the PLT section contents.  */
706:     plt_contents = (bfd_byte *)bfd_malloc(plt->size);
707:     if (plt_contents == NULL)
708:       break;
709:     if (!bfd_get_section_contents(abfd, (asection *)plt, plt_contents, 0,
710:                                   plt->size)) {
711:       free(plt_contents);
712:       break;
713:     }
714: 
715:     /* Check what kind of PLT it is.  */
716:     plt_type = plt_unknown;
717:     if (plts[j].type == plt_unknown &&
718:         (plt->size >= (lazy_plt->plt_entry_size + lazy_plt->plt_entry_size))) {
719:       /* Match lazy PLT first.  Need to check the first two
720:          instructions.   */
721:       if ((memcmp(plt_contents, lazy_plt->plt0_entry,
722:                   lazy_plt->plt0_got1_offset) == 0) &&
723:           (memcmp(plt_contents + 6, lazy_plt->plt0_entry + 6, 2) == 0))
724:         plt_type = plt_lazy;
725:       else if (lazy_bnd_plt != NULL &&
726:                (memcmp(plt_contents, lazy_bnd_plt->plt0_entry,
727:                        lazy_bnd_plt->plt0_got1_offset) == 0) &&
728:                (memcmp(plt_contents + 6, lazy_bnd_plt->plt0_entry + 6, 3) ==
729:                 0)) {
730:         plt_type = plt_lazy | plt_second;
731:         /* The fist entry in the lazy IBT PLT is the same as the
732:            lazy BND PLT.  */
733:         if ((memcmp(plt_contents + lazy_ibt_plt->plt_entry_size,
734:                     lazy_ibt_plt->plt_entry,
735:                     lazy_ibt_plt->plt_got_offset) == 0))
736:           lazy_plt = lazy_ibt_plt;
737:         else
738:           lazy_plt = lazy_bnd_plt;
739:       }
740:     }
741: 
742:     if (non_lazy_plt != NULL &&
743:         (plt_type == plt_unknown || plt_type == plt_non_lazy) &&
744:         plt->size >= non_lazy_plt->plt_entry_size) {
745:       /* Match non-lazy PLT.  */
746:       if (memcmp(plt_contents, non_lazy_plt->plt_entry,
747:                  non_lazy_plt->plt_got_offset) == 0)
748:         plt_type = plt_non_lazy;
749:     }
750: 
751:     if (plt_type == plt_unknown || plt_type == plt_second) {
752:       if (non_lazy_bnd_plt != NULL &&

591: /* Original code from bad.c */
592: long _bfd_elf_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc(bfd *abfd, arelent **storage,
593:                                          asymbol **syms) {
594:   bfd_boolean (*slurp_relocs)(bfd *, asection *, asymbol **, bfd_boolean);
595:   asection *s;
596:   long ret;
597: 
598:   if (elf_dynsymtab(abfd) == 0) {
599:     bfd_set_error(bfd_error_invalid_operation);
600:     return -1;
601:   }
602: 
603:   slurp_relocs = get_elf_backend_data(abfd)->s->slurp_reloc_table;
604:   ret = 0;
605:   for (s = abfd->sections; s != NULL; s = s->next) {
606:     if (elf_section_data(s)->this_hdr.sh_link == elf_dynsymtab(abfd) &&
607:         (elf_section_data(s)->this_hdr.sh_type == SHT_REL ||
608:          elf_section_data(s)->this_hdr.sh_type == SHT_RELA)) {
609:       arelent *p;
610:       long count, i;
611: 
612:       if (!(*slurp_relocs)(abfd, s, syms, TRUE))
613:         return -1;
614:       count = s->size / elf_section_data(s)->this_hdr.sh_entsize;
615:       p = s->relocation;
616:       for (i = 0; i < count; i++)
617:         *storage++ = p++;
618:       ret += count;
619:     }
620:   }
621: 
622:   *storage = NULL;
623: 
624:   return ret;
625: }

632:   long size, count, i, n;

635:   asymbol *s;

645:   asection *plt;
646:   char *names;
647:   enum elf_x86_64_plt_type plt_type;
648:   struct elf_x86_64_plt plts[] = {
649:       {".plt", NULL, NULL, plt_unknown, 0, 0, 0, 0},
650:       {".plt.got", NULL, NULL, plt_non_lazy, 0, 0, 0, 0},
651:       {".plt.sec", NULL, NULL, plt_second, 0, 0, 0, 0},
652:       {".plt.bnd", NULL, NULL, plt_second, 0, 0, 0, 0},
653:       {NULL, NULL, NULL, plt_non_lazy, 0, 0, 0, 0}};
654: 
655:   *ret = NULL;
656: 
657:   if ((abfd->flags & (DYNAMIC | EXEC_P)) == 0)
658:     return 0;
659: 
660:   if (dynsymcount <= 0)
661:     return 0;
662: 
663:   relsize = bfd_get_dynamic_reloc_upper_bound(abfd);
664:   if (relsize <= 0)
665:     return -1;
666: 
667:   dynrelbuf = (arelent **)bfd_malloc(relsize);
668:   if (dynrelbuf == NULL)
669:     return -1;
670: 
671:   dynrelcount = bfd_canonicalize_dynamic_reloc(abfd, dynrelbuf, dynsyms);
672:   if (dynrelcount <= 0)
673:     return -1;
674: 
675:   /* Sort the relocs by address.  */
676:   qsort(dynrelbuf, dynrelcount, sizeof(arelent *), compare_relocs);
677: 
678:   if (get_elf_x86_64_backend_data(abfd)->os == is_normal) {
679:     lazy_plt = &elf_x86_64_lazy_plt;
680:     non_lazy_plt = &elf_x86_64_non_lazy_plt;
681:     lazy_bnd_plt = &elf_x86_64_lazy_bnd_plt;
682:     non_lazy_bnd_plt = &elf_x86_64_non_lazy_bnd_plt;
683:     if (ABI_64_P(abfd)) {
684:       lazy_ibt_plt = &elf_x86_64_lazy_ibt_plt;
685:       non_lazy_ibt_plt = &elf_x86_64_non_lazy_ibt_plt;
686:     } else {
687:       lazy_ibt_plt = &elf_x32_lazy_ibt_plt;
688:       non_lazy_ibt_plt = &elf_x32_non_lazy_ibt_plt;
689:     }
690:   } else {
691:     lazy_plt = &elf_x86_64_nacl_plt;
692:     non_lazy_plt = NULL;
693:     lazy_bnd_plt = NULL;
694:     non_lazy_bnd_plt = NULL;
695:     lazy_ibt_plt = NULL;
696:     non_lazy_ibt_plt = NULL;
697:   }
698: 
699:   count = 0;
700:   for (j = 0; plts[j].name != NULL; j++) {
701:     plt = bfd_get_section_by_name(abfd, plts[j].name);
702:     if (plt == NULL || plt->size == 0)
703:       continue;
704: 
705:     /* Get the PLT section contents.  */
706:     plt_contents = (bfd_byte *)bfd_malloc(plt->size);
707:     if (plt_contents == NULL)
708:       break;
709:     if (!bfd_get_section_contents(abfd, (asection *)plt, plt_contents, 0,
710:                                   plt->size)) {
711:       free(plt_contents);
712:       break;
713:     }
714: 
715:     /* Check what kind of PLT it is.  */
716:     plt_type = plt_unknown;
717:     if (plts[j].type == plt_unknown &&
718:         (plt->size >= (lazy_plt->plt_entry_size + lazy_plt->plt_entry_size))) {
719:       /* Match lazy PLT first.  Need to check the first two
720:          instructions.   */
721:       if ((memcmp(plt_contents, lazy_plt->plt0_entry,
722:                   lazy_plt->plt0_got1_offset) == 0) &&
723:           (memcmp(plt_contents + 6, lazy_plt->plt0_entry + 6, 2) == 0))
724:         plt_type = plt_lazy;
725:       else if (lazy_bnd_plt != NULL &&
726:                (memcmp(plt_contents, lazy_bnd_plt->plt0_entry,
727:                        lazy_bnd_plt->plt0_got1_offset) == 0) &&
728:                (memcmp(plt_contents + 6, lazy_bnd_plt->plt0_entry + 6, 3) ==
729:                 0)) {
730:         plt_type = plt_lazy | plt_second;
731:         /* The fist entry in the lazy IBT PLT is the same as the
732:            lazy BND PLT.  */
733:         if ((memcmp(plt_contents + lazy_ibt_plt->plt_entry_size,
734:                     lazy_ibt_plt->plt_entry,
735:                     lazy_ibt_plt->plt_got_offset) == 0))
736:           lazy_plt = lazy_ibt_plt;
737:         else
738:           lazy_plt = lazy_bnd_plt;
739:       }
740:     }
741: 
742:     if (non_lazy_plt != NULL &&
743:         (plt_type == plt_unknown || plt_type == plt_non_lazy) &&
744:         plt->size >= non_lazy_plt->plt_entry_size) {
745:       /* Match non-lazy PLT.  */
746:       if (memcmp(plt_contents, non_lazy_plt->plt_entry,
747:                  non_lazy_plt->plt_got_offset) == 0)
748:         plt_type = plt_non_lazy;
749:     }
750: 
751:     if (plt_type == plt_unknown || plt_type == plt_second) {
752:       if (non_lazy_bnd_plt != NULL &&
753:           plt->size >= non_lazy_bnd_plt->plt_entry_size &&
754:           (memcmp(plt_contents, non_lazy_bnd_plt->plt_entry,
755:                   non_lazy_bnd_plt->plt_got_offset) == 0)) {
756:         /* Match BND PLT.  */
757:         plt_type = plt_second;
758:         non_lazy_plt = non_lazy_bnd_plt;
759:       } else if (non_lazy_ibt_plt != NULL &&
760:                  plt->size >= non_lazy_ibt_plt->plt_entry_size &&
761:                  (memcmp(plt_contents, non_lazy_ibt_plt->plt_entry,
762:                          non_lazy_ibt_plt->plt_got_offset) == 0)) {
763:         /* Match IBT PLT.  */
764:         plt_type = plt_second;
765:         non_lazy_plt = non_lazy_ibt_plt;
766:       }
767:     }
768: 
769:     if (plt_type == plt_unknown)
770:       continue;
771: 
772:     plts[j].sec = plt;
773:     plts[j].type = plt_type;
774: 
775:     if ((plt_type & plt_lazy)) {
776:       plts[j].plt_got_offset = lazy_plt->plt_got_offset;
777:       plts[j].plt_got_insn_size = lazy_plt->plt_got_insn_size;
778:       plts[j].plt_entry_size = lazy_plt->plt_entry_size;
779:       /* Skip PLT0 in lazy PLT.  */
780:       i = 1;
781:     } else {
782:       plts[j].plt_got_offset = non_lazy_plt->plt_got_offset;
783:       plts[j].plt_got_insn_size = non_lazy_plt->plt_got_insn_size;
784:       plts[j].plt_entry_size = non_lazy_plt->plt_entry_size;
785:       i = 0;
786:     }
787: 
788:     /* Skip lazy PLT when the second PLT is used.  */
789:     if (plt_type == (plt_lazy | plt_second))
790:       plts[j].count = 0;
791:     else {
792:       n = plt->size / plts[j].plt_entry_size;
793:       plts[j].count = n;
794:       count += n - i;
795:     }
796: 
797:     plts[j].contents = plt_contents;
798:   }
799: 
800:   if (count == 0)
801:     return -1;
802: 
803:   size = count * sizeof(asymbol);
804:   s = *ret = (asymbol *)bfd_zmalloc(size);
805:   if (s == NULL) {
806:   bad_return:
807:     for (j = 0; plts[j].name != NULL; j++)
808:       if (plts[j].contents != NULL)
809:         free(plts[j].contents);
810:     free(dynrelbuf);
811:     return -1;
812:   }
813: 
814:   /* Check for each PLT section.  */
815:   size = 0;
816:   n = 0;
817:   for (j = 0; plts[j].name != NULL; j++)
818:     if ((plt_contents = plts[j].contents) != NULL) {
819:       long k;
820:       bfd_vma offset;
821: 
822:       plt_got_offset = plts[j].plt_got_offset;
823:       plt_got_insn_size = plts[j].plt_got_insn_size;
824:       plt_entry_size = plts[j].plt_entry_size;
825: 
826:       plt = plts[j].sec;
827: 
828:       if ((plts[j].type & plt_lazy)) {
829:         /* Skip PLT0 in lazy PLT.  */
830:         k = 1;
831:         offset = plt_entry_size;
832:       } else {
833:         k = 0;
834:         offset = 0;
835:       }
836: 
837:       /* Check each PLT entry against dynamic relocations.  */
838:       for (; k < plts[j].count; k++) {
839:         int off;
840:         bfd_vma got_vma;
841:         long min, max, mid;
842:         arelent *p;
843: 
844:         /* Get the PC-relative offset, a signed 32-bit integer.  */
845:         off = H_GET_32(abfd, (plt_contents + offset + plt_got_offset));
846:         got_vma = plt->vma + offset + off + plt_got_insn_size;
847: 
848:         /* Binary search.  */
849:         p = dynrelbuf[0];
850:         min = 0;
851:         max = dynrelcount;
852:         while ((min + 1) < max) {
853:           arelent *r;
854: 
855:           mid = (min + max) / 2;
856:           r = dynrelbuf[mid];
857:           if (got_vma > r->address)
858:             min = mid;
859:           else if (got_vma < r->address)
860:             max = mid;
861:           else {
862:             p = r;
863:             break;
864:           }
865:         }
866: 
867:         /* Skip unknown relocation.  PR 17512: file: bc9d6cf5.  */
868:         if (got_vma == p->address && p->howto != NULL &&
869:             (p->howto->type == R_X86_64_JUMP_SLOT ||
870:              p->howto->type == R_X86_64_GLOB_DAT ||
871:              p->howto->type == R_X86_64_IRELATIVE)) {
872:           *s = **p->sym_ptr_ptr;
873:           /* Undefined syms won't have BSF_LOCAL or BSF_GLOBAL
874:              set.  Since we are defining a symbol, ensure one
875:              of them is set.  */
876:           if ((s->flags & BSF_LOCAL) == 0)
877:             s->flags |= BSF_GLOBAL;
878:           s->flags |= BSF_SYNTHETIC;
879:           /* This is no longer a section symbol.  */
880:           s->flags &= ~BSF_SECTION_SYM;
881:           s->section = plt;
882:           s->the_bfd = plt->owner;
883:           s->value = offset;
884:           /* Store relocation for later use.  */
885:           s->udata.p = p;
886:           /* Add @plt to function name later.  */
887:           size += strlen(s->name) + sizeof("@plt");
888:           if (p->addend != 0)
889:             size += sizeof("+0x") - 1 + 8 + 8 * ABI_64_P(abfd);
890:           n++;
891:           s++;
892:         }
893:         offset += plt_entry_size;
894:       }
895:     }
896: 
897:   /* PLT entries with R_X86_64_TLSDESC relocations are skipped.  */
898:   if (n == 0)
899:     goto bad_return;
900: 
901:   count = n;
902: 
903:   /* Allocate space for @plt suffixes.  */
904:   names = (char *)bfd_malloc(size);
905:   if (s == NULL)
906:     goto bad_return;
907: 
908:   s = *ret;
909:   for (i = 0; i < count; i++) {
910:     /* Add @plt to function name.  */
911:     arelent *p = (arelent *)s->udata.p;
912:     /* Clear it now.  */
913:     s->udata.p = NULL;
914:     size = strlen(s->name);
915:     memcpy(names, s->name, size);
916:     s->name = names;
917:     names += size;
918:     if (p->addend != 0) {
919:       char buf[30], *a;
920: 
921:       memcpy(names, "+0x", sizeof("+0x") - 1);
922:       names += sizeof("+0x") - 1;
923:       bfd_sprintf_vma(abfd, buf, p->addend);
924:       for (a = buf; *a == '0'; ++a)
925:         ;
926:       size = strlen(a);
927:       memcpy(names, a, size);
928:       names += size;
929:     }
930:     memcpy(names, "@plt", sizeof("@plt"));
931:     names += sizeof("@plt");
932:     s++;
933:   }
934: 
935:   for (j = 0; plts[j].name != NULL; j++)
936:     if (plts[j].contents != NULL)
937:       free(plts[j].contents);
938: 
939:   free(dynrelbuf);
940: 
941:   return count;
942: }

944: static void dump_bfd(bfd *abfd) {
945:   /* If we are adjusting section VMA's, change them all now.  Changing
946:      the BFD information is a hack.  However, we must do it, or
947:      bfd_find_nearest_line will not do the right thing.  */
948:   if (adjust_section_vma != 0) {
949:     bfd_boolean has_reloc = (abfd->flags & HAS_RELOC);
950:     bfd_map_over_sections(abfd, adjust_addresses, &has_reloc);
951:   }
952: 
953:   if (!dump_debugging_tags && !suppress_bfd_header)
954:     printf(_("\n%s:     file format %s\n"), bfd_get_filename(abfd),
955:            abfd->xvec->name);
956:   if (dump_ar_hdrs)
957:     print_arelt_descr(stdout, abfd, TRUE);
958:   if (dump_file_header)
959:     dump_bfd_header(abfd);
960:   if (dump_private_headers)
961:     dump_bfd_private_header(abfd);
962:   if (dump_private_options != NULL)
963:     dump_target_specific(abfd);
964:   if (!dump_debugging_tags && !suppress_bfd_header)
965:     putchar('\n');
966: 
967:   if (dump_symtab || dump_reloc_info || disassemble || dump_debugging ||
968:       dump_dwarf_section_info)
969:     syms = slurp_symtab(abfd);
970: 
971:   if (dump_section_headers)
972:     dump_headers(abfd);
973: 
974:   if (dump_dynamic_symtab || dump_dynamic_reloc_info ||
975:       (disassemble && bfd_get_dynamic_symtab_upper_bound(abfd) > 0))
976:     dynsyms = slurp_dynamic_symtab(abfd);
977:   if (disassemble) {
978:     synthcount = bfd_get_synthetic_symtab(abfd, symcount, syms, dynsymcount,
979:                                           dynsyms, &synthsyms);
980:     if (synthcount < 0)
981:       synthcount = 0;
982:   }
983: 
984:   if (dump_symtab)
985:     dump_symbols(abfd, FALSE);
986:   if (dump_dynamic_symtab)
987:     dump_symbols(abfd, TRUE);
988:   if (dump_dwarf_section_info)
989:     dump_dwarf(abfd);
990:   if (dump_stab_section_info)
991:     dump_stabs(abfd);
992:   if (dump_reloc_info && !disassemble)
993:     dump_relocs(abfd);
994:   if (dump_dynamic_reloc_info && !disassemble)
995:     dump_dynamic_relocs(abfd);
996:   if (dump_section_contents)
997:     dump_data(abfd);
998:   if (disassemble)
999:     disassemble_data(abfd);
1000: 
1001:   if (dump_debugging) {
1002:     void *dhandle;
1003: 
1004:     dhandle = read_debugging_info(abfd, syms, symcount, TRUE);
1005:     if (dhandle != NULL) {
1006:       if (!print_debugging_info(stdout, dhandle, abfd, syms, bfd_demangle,
1007:                                 dump_debugging_tags ? TRUE : FALSE)) {
1008:         non_fatal(_("%s: printing debugging information failed"),
1009:                   bfd_get_filename(abfd));
1010:         exit_status = 1;
1011:       }
1012:     }
1013:     /* PR 6483: If there was no STABS or IEEE debug
1014:        info in the file, try DWARF instead.  */
1015:     else if (!dump_dwarf_section_info) {
1016:       dwarf_select_sections_all();
1017:       dump_dwarf(abfd);
1018:     }
1019:   }
1020: 
1021:   if (syms) {
1022:     free(syms);
1023:     syms = NULL;
1024:   }
1025: 
1026:   if (dynsyms) {
1027:     free(dynsyms);
1028:     dynsyms = NULL;
1029:   }
1030: 
1031:   if (synthsyms) {
1032:     free(synthsyms);
1033:     synthsyms = NULL;
1034:   }
1035: 
1036:   symcount = 0;
1037:   dynsymcount = 0;
1038:   synthcount = 0;
1039: }

1041: static void display_object_bfd(bfd *abfd) {
1042:   char **matching;
1043: 
1044:   if (bfd_check_format_matches(abfd, bfd_object, &matching)) {
1045:     dump_bfd(abfd);
1046:     return;
1047:   }
1048: 
1049:   if (bfd_get_error() == bfd_error_file_ambiguously_recognized) {
1050:     nonfatal(bfd_get_filename(abfd));
1051:     list_matching_formats(matching);
1052:     free(matching);
1053:     return;
1054:   }
1055: 
1056:   if (bfd_get_error() != bfd_error_file_not_recognized) {
1057:     nonfatal(bfd_get_filename(abfd));
1058:     return;
1059:   }
1060: 
1061:   if (bfd_check_format_matches(abfd, bfd_core, &matching)) {
1062:     dump_bfd(abfd);
1063:     return;
1064:   }
1065: 
1066:   nonfatal(bfd_get_filename(abfd));
1067: 
1068:   if (bfd_get_error() == bfd_error_file_ambiguously_recognized) {
1069:     list_matching_formats(matching);
1070:     free(matching);
1071:   }
1072: }

1074: static void display_any_bfd(bfd *file, int level) {
1075:   /* Decompress sections unless dumping the section contents.  */
1076:   if (!dump_section_contents)
1077:     file->flags |= BFD_DECOMPRESS;
1078: 
1079:   /* If the file is an archive, process all of its elements.  */
1080:   if (bfd_check_format(file, bfd_archive)) {
1081:     bfd *arfile = NULL;
1082:     bfd *last_arfile = NULL;
1083: 
1084:     if (level == 0)
1085:       printf(_("In archive %s:\n"), bfd_get_filename(file));
1086:     else if (level > 100) {
1087:       /* Prevent corrupted files from spinning us into an
1088:          infinite loop.  100 is an arbitrary heuristic.  */
1089:       fatal(_("Archive nesting is too deep"));
1090:       return;
1091:     } else
1092:       printf(_("In nested archive %s:\n"), bfd_get_filename(file));
1093: 
1094:     for (;;) {
1095:       bfd_set_error(bfd_error_no_error);
1096: 
1097:       arfile = bfd_openr_next_archived_file(file, arfile);
1098:       if (arfile == NULL) {
1099:         if (bfd_get_error() != bfd_error_no_more_archived_files)
1100:           nonfatal(bfd_get_filename(file));
1101:         break;
1102:       }
1103: 
1104:       display_any_bfd(arfile, level + 1);
1105: 
1106:       if (last_arfile != NULL) {
1107:         bfd_close(last_arfile);
1108:         /* PR 17512: file: ac585d01.  */
1109:         if (arfile == last_arfile) {
1110:           last_arfile = NULL;
1111:           break;
1112:         }
1113:       }
1114:       last_arfile = arfile;
1115:     }
1116: 
1117:     if (last_arfile != NULL)
1118:       bfd_close(last_arfile);
1119:   } else
1120:     display_object_bfd(file);
1121: }

1123: const bfd_target *bfd_find_target(const char *target_name, bfd *abfd) {
1124:   const char *targname;
1125:   const bfd_target *target;
1126: 
1127:   if (target_name != NULL)
1128:     targname = target_name;
1129:   else
1130:     targname = getenv("GNUTARGET");
1131: 
1132:   /* This is safe; the vector cannot be null.  */
1133:   if (targname == NULL || strcmp(targname, "default") == 0) {
1134:     if (bfd_default_vector[0] != NULL)
1135:       target = bfd_default_vector[0];
1136:     else
1137:       target = bfd_target_vector[0];
1138:     if (abfd) {
1139:       abfd->xvec = target;
1140:       abfd->target_defaulted = TRUE;
1141:     }
1142:     return target;
1143:   }
1144: 
1145:   if (abfd)
1146:     abfd->target_defaulted = FALSE;
1147: 
1148:   target = find_target(targname);
1149:   if (target == NULL)
1150:     return NULL;
1151: 
1152:   if (abfd)
1153:     abfd->xvec = target;
1154:   return target;
1155: }

1157: bfd *bfd_fopen(const char *filename, const char *target, const char *mode,
1158:                int fd) {
1159:   bfd *nbfd;
1160:   const bfd_target *target_vec;
1161: 
1162:   nbfd = _bfd_new_bfd();
1163:   if (nbfd == NULL) {
1164:     if (fd != -1)
1165:       close(fd);
1166:     return NULL;
1167:   }
1168: 
1169:   target_vec = bfd_find_target(target, nbfd);
1170:   if (target_vec == NULL) {
1171:     if (fd != -1)
1172:       close(fd);
1173:     _bfd_delete_bfd(nbfd);
1174:     return NULL;
1175:   }
1176: 
1177: #ifdef HAVE_FDOPEN
1178:   if (fd != -1)
1179:     nbfd->iostream = fdopen(fd, mode);
1180:   else
1181: #endif
1182:     nbfd->iostream = _bfd_real_fopen(filename, mode);
1183:   if (nbfd->iostream == NULL) {
1184:     bfd_set_error(bfd_error_system_call);
1185:     _bfd_delete_bfd(nbfd);
1186:     return NULL;
1187:   }
1188: 
1189:   /* OK, put everything where it belongs.  */
1190: 
1191:   /* PR 11983: Do not cache the original filename, but
1192:      rather make a copy - the original might go away.  */
1193:   nbfd->filename = xstrdup(filename);
1194: 
1195:   /* Figure out whether the user is opening the file for reading,
1196:      writing, or both, by looking at the MODE argument.  */
1197:   if ((mode[0] == 'r' || mode[0] == 'w' || mode[0] == 'a') && mode[1] == '+')
1198:     nbfd->direction = both_direction;
1199:   else if (mode[0] == 'r')
1200:     nbfd->direction = read_direction;
1201:   else
1202:     nbfd->direction = write_direction;
1203: 
1204:   if (!bfd_cache_init(nbfd)) {
1205:     _bfd_delete_bfd(nbfd);
1206:     return NULL;
1207:   }
1208:   nbfd->opened_once = TRUE;
1209: 
1210:   /* If we opened the file by name, mark it cacheable; we can close it
1211:      and reopen it later.  However, if a file descriptor was provided,
1212:      then it may have been opened with special flags that make it
1213:      unsafe to close and reopen the file.  */
